program_information.program_details: Comprehensive Management of Chronic Constipation in Primary Care: A Case-Based CME Symposium Series
program_information.needs_assessment_summary:
Chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC) and constipation-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-C) are common yet frequently underdiagnosed and undertreated conditions in the primary care setting. Our internal chart review at Midtown Community Health Network (MCHN) across three primary care clinics in 2024 showed that approximately 14% of adult patients had documentation of chronic constipation symptoms, but only 38% of those had a formal diagnosis recorded in the problem list. Even more concerning, only 22% of patients with persistent symptoms beyond 6 months had been referred to gastroenterology or offered evidence-based pharmacologic options beyond over-the-counter laxatives.

A brief survey we conducted in May 2024 with 76 primary care clinicians (family medicine, internal medicine, and nurse practitioners) at MCHN revealed several key educational gaps:
- 64% reported feeling "somewhat" or "not very" confident distinguishing CIC from IBS-C based on current diagnostic criteria.
- 58% were unaware of the most recent American College of Gastroenterology guideline recommendations for stepwise management of chronic constipation.
- 71% reported that they "rarely" or "never" used validated symptom or quality-of-life assessment tools for constipation.
- 47% expressed uncertainty about when to escalate treatment beyond lifestyle modifications and osmotic laxatives, leading to prolonged symptom burden for patients.

Additionally, patient satisfaction data from our 2023 Press Ganey reports and internal complaints highlight that many patients feel their constipation concerns are dismissed or minimized. Several patients explicitly commented that they "felt embarrassed" to bring up bowel issues, or that clinicians "just told me to eat more fiber" without a structured plan or follow-up. This indicates a need not only for updated clinical knowledge but also for improved communication strategies and shared decision-making around sensitive GI topics.

Given the increasing availability of newer prescription agents (e.g., secretagogues and prokinetic agents) and evolving evidence regarding long-term safety and effectiveness, our clinicians have requested structured, case-based education that walks through realistic scenarios: older adults with multiple comorbidities and polypharmacy, pre-menopausal women with IBS-C features, and patients with overlapping pelvic floor dysfunction. The proposed program directly addresses these documented needs by providing practical, guideline-aligned strategies, tools for assessment, and communication techniques tailored to a busy primary care environment serving a predominantly urban, diverse patient population.
program_information.learning_objectives:
By the end of this CME program, participants will be able to:

1. Accurately differentiate between chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC) and constipation-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-C) using current diagnostic criteria, including Rome IV criteria, and apply this differentiation in at least two common primary care patient scenarios.

2. Implement a stepwise, evidence-based treatment algorithm for adults with chronic constipation, beginning with lifestyle and dietary interventions, appropriately timed use of over-the-counter agents, and escalation to prescription therapies when clinically indicated.

3. Identify red-flag symptoms and patient characteristics that warrant further diagnostic evaluation or referral to gastroenterology, such as unintentional weight loss, anemia, rectal bleeding, or family history of colorectal cancer.

4. Utilize at least one validated symptom assessment tool and one patient-reported outcome measure (e.g., PAC-SYM, PAC-QOL) to monitor response to therapy and guide shared decision-making.

5. Demonstrate effective, empathetic communication techniques for discussing bowel habits and constipation with patients who may feel embarrassed or stigmatized, including strategies to normalize the conversation and engage patients from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds.

6. Integrate multidisciplinary resources (e.g., nutrition services, pelvic floor physical therapy, behavioral health) into individualized care plans for patients with complex or refractory constipation to improve symptom control and quality of life.
program_information.program_activity_description:
This program will be delivered as a blended educational initiative combining three live, in-person CME dinner symposia with a complementary on-demand enduring online module. The target audience is primary care clinicians (family physicians, internists, nurse practitioners, and physician assistants) practicing within and around the Midtown Community Health Network (MCHN) in the greater Chicago area.

1. Live CME Dinner Symposia (3 events):
- Format: 2.5-hour evening sessions (6:00–8:30 PM) combining didactic presentations, interactive case discussions, and small-group breakouts.
- Agenda (typical):
  • 6:00–6:30 PM: Registration and light dinner, distribution of pre-activity surveys and baseline knowledge/self-efficacy assessments.
  • 6:30–7:00 PM: Plenary lecture by a board-certified gastroenterologist on the epidemiology, pathophysiology, and diagnostic framework for chronic constipation in adults.
  • 7:00–7:45 PM: Case-based interactive session led jointly by a gastroenterologist and a primary care physician. Cases will include: (a) a 68-year-old woman with multiple comorbidities and polypharmacy; (b) a 35-year-old woman with IBS-C and overlapping anxiety; (c) a 52-year-old man with new-onset constipation and alarm features.
  • 7:45–8:15 PM: Small-group breakouts (6–8 learners per table) discussing practical implementation challenges, patient communication techniques, and local referral pathways. Each table will be facilitated by trained faculty using standardized discussion guides.
  • 8:15–8:30 PM: Summary of key takeaways, Q&A, and completion of post-activity evaluations and commitment-to-change statements.
- Faculty: Each event will feature 1 gastroenterologist, 1 primary care physician, and 1 advanced practice provider (NP or PA) with experience managing chronic constipation.

2. Enduring Online Module:
- A 45–60 minute self-paced module hosted on the MCHN learning management system. It will repurpose content from the live sessions, including recorded segments (with all promotional content removed), updated slides, and interactive knowledge checks.
- The module will be accessible for 12 months following launch and will be advertised to clinicians who cannot attend live events.

3. Educational Materials and Tools:
- Printable and electronic algorithms outlining stepwise management of chronic constipation.
- Patient-facing handouts on diet, fiber, and lifestyle modifications in English and Spanish.
- Templates for EHR smart phrases to facilitate documentation and patient counseling.

4. Accreditation and Compliance:
- The activity will be planned and implemented in accordance with ACCME standards for independence and integrity in accredited continuing education. All content will be fair-balanced, evidence-based, and free from commercial bias. Faculty will disclose all relevant financial relationships with ineligible companies, and any conflicts will be mitigated prior to the activity.

5. Logistics:
- Events will be held at centrally located conference facilities with easy access to public transportation and parking. Registration will be managed through the MCHN CME office, with pre-registration required for capacity planning. Dietary accommodations will be offered upon request.

Overall, the program is designed to be practical, interactive, and directly applicable to day-to-day primary care practice, with a strong emphasis on real-world cases and implementation strategies rather than purely theoretical content.
program_information.methods_measure_educational_outcomes_pat:
We will use a multi-level outcomes framework aligned with Moore’s Levels of Outcomes to evaluate both educational effectiveness and potential impact on patient care.

1. Participation (Level 1):
- Track the number of unique learners attending each live session and completing the enduring online module. Attendance will be captured via sign-in sheets (live) and LMS analytics (online). We will monitor profession (MD/DO, NP, PA, RN), specialty, and practice site.

2. Satisfaction (Level 2):
- Immediately following each live event and at the completion of the online module, participants will complete a standardized satisfaction survey. Questions will assess perceived relevance, balance, clarity of presentations, usefulness of case discussions, and likelihood of recommending the activity to colleagues.

3. Learning – Knowledge and Competence (Levels 3a and 3b):
- Pre- and post-activity multiple-choice questions (5–7 items) will assess knowledge of diagnostic criteria, treatment algorithms, and red-flag symptoms.
- Clinical vignettes embedded in the evaluation will assess changes in competence (e.g., choosing appropriate next steps in management for specific patient scenarios).
- We will calculate effect sizes and percentage of learners demonstrating an improvement of at least 20 percentage points between pre- and post-tests.

4. Performance – Changes in Clinical Practice (Level 4):
- At 8–12 weeks post-activity, participants will receive an online follow-up survey asking them to report on:
  • Which specific practice changes they implemented (e.g., routine use of a constipation assessment tool, earlier initiation of prescription therapies, more systematic screening for alarm features).
  • Perceived barriers and facilitators to implementing these changes.
- We will also conduct a limited chart review (approximately 10 charts per participating clinician, stratified sample) at two MCHN clinics that have agreed to participate in quality improvement. We will examine documentation of constipation diagnosis, symptom assessment, stepwise treatment, and appropriate referrals before and after the program.

5. Patient/Health Outcomes (Level 5, exploratory):
- For the subset of clinicians participating in the chart review, we will track proxy patient outcome measures over a 6-month period, such as:
  • Frequency of documented improvement in bowel habits.
  • Reduction in emergency department visits for severe constipation or fecal impaction.
  • Patient-reported satisfaction with management of bowel symptoms (using a brief 3-item questionnaire administered in the waiting room or via patient portal).

6. Continuous Improvement:
- Evaluation data will be reviewed quarterly by the MCHN CME Committee. Content and cases for the enduring module may be updated based on learner feedback (e.g., adding more content about managing constipation in patients with opioid use, or in those with diabetes-related neuropathy).
- A brief outcomes summary report will be shared with faculty and organizational leadership, highlighting key findings, areas for improvement, and opportunities for future related programs (e.g., follow-up activities focusing on pelvic floor dysfunction).

All data will be de-identified and aggregated for reporting. IRB consultation will be sought to ensure that chart review and patient outcome assessments are conducted in compliance with ethical and privacy standards.
program_information.list_other_potential_supporters:
We have approached two additional potential supporters to partially offset program costs and broaden the sustainability of the educational initiative:

1. Chicago Community Health Foundation (CCHF):
   - A local nonprofit foundation that provides small grants to support quality improvement and continuing education initiatives in safety-net clinics across Cook County.
   - We have submitted a request for $10,000 to cover a portion of the enduring online module development costs and translation of patient education materials into Spanish and Polish.

2. Lakeshore Medical Education Consortium (LMEC):
   - A regional consortium of hospitals and academic medical centers that occasionally co-sponsors CME events focused on primary care topics.
   - We have had preliminary discussions about a possible in-kind contribution, such as marketing support through their newsletter and access to their webinar platform, as well as a modest financial contribution of approximately $5,000.

At the time of this application, no additional financial support has been confirmed; all amounts are pending review by the respective organizations. If other supporters are secured, we will ensure full transparency and compliance with all applicable guidelines, including independent decision-making around content and faculty selection.
program_information.disclose_relationship_ironwood_organizat:
Midtown Community Health Network (MCHN) does not have any direct legal, financial, or contractual relationships with Ironwood beyond prior independent educational grants. In 2022, MCHN received an unrestricted educational grant from Ironwood to support a separate CME program on irritable bowel syndrome; that activity was planned and implemented independently in accordance with ACCME standards, with Ironwood having no role in content development, faculty selection, or materials review.

MCHN has no ownership interest in Ironwood, and no individuals in our CME office or leadership team serve on Ironwood’s boards or advisory committees. A small number of our proposed faculty members have, in the past, served as speakers or consultants for various pharmaceutical companies, including Ironwood; all such relationships will be fully disclosed, reviewed, and mitigated according to our standard conflict-of-interest policies prior to their participation in this activity.

Aside from these prior educational grants and standard disclosure-managed faculty relationships, there are no other business, research, or promotional arrangements between Ironwood and MCHN. We will maintain complete independence of content, and Ironwood will not have input into topic selection, slide development, or participant recruitment for this program.
